title: Asperula tetraphylla
taxonomy: Asperula tetraphylla is classified in the family Rubiaceae, commonly known as the coffee or madder family. This family comprises of flowering plants, which are predominantly woody and have a characteristic inflorescence. The genus Asperula belongs to the tribe Rubieae and comprises of about 100 species of flowering plants. Asperula tetraphylla is a specific species of the genus Asperula, characterized by its four-leafed leaves and unique growth habit. The plant's classification is further supported by its floral morphology and leaf structure, which are typical of the Rubiaceae family.

constituents: Asperula tetraphylla, also known as Woodruff, contains a variety of active constituents that contribute to its medicinal properties. These constituents include iridoids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids, which have been found to exhibit antioxidant and anti-inflammatory activities. The plant's iridoids, such as asperuloside and isoverrucoside, have been shown to have potential in treating various health conditions, including gastrointestinal issues and cardiovascular disease. Additionally, the flavonoids present in Woodruff have been reported to have antimicrobial and anti-cancer properties. Further research is needed to fully understand the therapeutic potential of Asperula tetraphylla's active constituents.

intro: Asperula tetraphylla, also known as Woodruff or Wild Ginger, is a perennial herbaceous plant belonging to the Rubiaceae family. Asperula tetraphylla has been traditionally used for its medicinal properties, offering relief from digestive issues, reducing inflammation and anxiety, and acting as an antiseptic and antimicrobial agent. The bioactive constituents of Asperula tetraphylla include iridoid glycosides, phenolic acids, and flavonoids, which contribute to its therapeutic effects. The plant is commonly used to make herbal teas, infusions, and extracts, as well as being incorporated into tinctures and salves, which are often used to treat digestive issues and skin problems.
